content.explanation:
🧱 What is an HTML Element?

An HTML element is a complete building block of a webpage.

It is made of 3 parts:

  • 🔹 Opening tag
  • 🔹 Content
  • 🔹 Closing tag

📦 Simple Analogy

Think of elements like boxes:

  • 📦 Outer box = tag
  • 📄 Inside = content
  • 🔒 Closing = sealing the box

Every box must be properly closed or everything breaks.

🧠 Key Idea

HTML is like stacking boxes inside boxes in the correct order.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🔧 How Nesting Works

Nesting means putting one element inside another.

But there is ONE important rule:

⚠️ Inside-out rule

The last tag you open must be the first one you close.

<!-- ✅ Correct -->
<p>This is <strong>bold text</strong> inside a paragraph</p>

<!-- ❌ Wrong -->
<p>This is <strong>broken structure</p></strong>

If nesting is wrong, the browser gets confused and layout breaks.

editor_sandbox.starter_code: We are building an ecosystem with Cymor Tech Services

mini_challenge.instruction:
🎯 Mission: Fix the broken code

1. The tags are in the wrong order
2. Fix them so HTML follows correct nesting rules
3. Run the code and make sure it displays correctly
mini_challenge.hint:
👉 Correct structure should look like this:

Text Bold Text

Remember: close INNER tag first!

quiz_engine.question: What is the correct rule for nesting HTML elements?
options: Always close tags in the reverse order they were opened
It does not matter which tag closes first
Always close outer tags first before inner tags
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 HTML follows the inside-out rule. The last opened tag must always be the first one closed to keep structure valid.

takeaways: An element is made of opening tag, content, and closing tag
Nesting means placing elements inside other elements
HTML must follow inside-out closing order
Incorrect nesting breaks layout and structure

cheat_sheet.element: A full HTML structure unit (tag + content + tag)
cheat_sheet.nesting: Placing elements inside other elements safely
cheat_sheet.rule: Close inner tags before outer tags
